content.text: The manager-worker pattern introduces explicit capacity management: a pool of reusable worker agents accepts tasks from a queue managed by the manager. Unlike the supervisor (which routes turn-by-turn) or the orchestrator (which fans out a plan), the manager tracks worker availability, assigns tasks as capacity opens, and collects results as workers finish. This is the right pattern when task volume is high, tasks are uniform, and throughput matters more than custom per-task routing.

attrs.alt: Manager-worker pattern: manager holds task queue, assigns to idle workers in a pool, collects results

content.text: — classify 500 documents, translate 1000 strings, summarize a corpus of articles.
— pool workers cap concurrent API calls to stay within per-minute token budgets.
— every task requires the same worker capability; no per-task routing logic needed.
— you want N tasks running simultaneously, not an unbounded fan-out.
For pure Python batch jobs outside LangGraph, asyncio's
is the simplest capacity control: it limits how many coroutines can call the LLM simultaneously without building a queue class.

import asyncio
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Any

llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001")

@dataclass
class WorkItem:
    id: str
    payload: str

@dataclass
class WorkResult:
    id: str
    output: str
    error: str | None = None

async def process_item(
    item: WorkItem,
    sem: asyncio.Semaphore,
) -> WorkResult:
    """One worker: acquire semaphore slot, call LLM, release slot."""
    async with sem:
        try:
            resp = await llm.ainvoke([
                {"role": "system", "content": "Summarise the following text in one sentence."},
                {"role": "user",   "content": item.payload},
            ])
            return WorkResult(id=item.id, output=resp.content)
        except Exception as exc:
            return WorkResult(id=item.id, output="", error=str(exc))

async def manager(
    items: list[WorkItem],
    max_workers: int = 4,
) -> list[WorkResult]:
    """Manager: create one task per item, cap concurrency via semaphore."""
    sem = asyncio.Semaphore(max_workers)
    tasks = [process_item(item, sem) for item in items]
    results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
    return list(results)

# Usage
work = [WorkItem(id=str(i), payload=f"Long article #{i}...") for i in range(20)]
results = asyncio.run(manager(work, max_workers=5))
successes = [r for r in results if not r.error]
print(f"Completed {len(successes)}/{len(results)} items")

Inside a LangGraph pipeline, the manager node distributes tasks from shared state to workers via the Send API. The worker pool size is controlled by splitting the task list into batches rather than by a semaphore — each batch becomes one wave of parallel Send calls.

def build_manager_worker() -> StateGraph:
    builder = StateGraph(ManagerState)

    builder.add_node("manager",   manager_node)
    builder.add_node("worker",    worker_node)
    builder.add_node("aggregate", aggregate_node)

    builder.set_entry_point("manager")

    # Manager decides: dispatch more workers or aggregate
    builder.add_conditional_edges(
        "manager", has_pending,
        {"dispatch": "worker", "aggregate": "aggregate"},
    )

    # Each wave of workers fans out via Send; when done, route back to manager
    builder.add_conditional_edges(
        "manager",
        dispatch_batch,   # returns list[Send] on "dispatch" path
    )
    builder.add_edge("worker",    "manager")   # workers return to manager
    builder.add_edge("aggregate", END)

    return builder.compile()

pipeline = build_manager_worker()
result = pipeline.invoke({
    "tasks": [{"id": str(i), "payload": f"Article {i}"} for i in range(12)],
    "results": [], "errors": [], "final": "",
})
print(f"Processed {len(result['results'])} items")
print(result["final"][:200])
With large batches, observability is critical. Track how many tasks have completed and which have failed without waiting for the full pipeline to finish.

import time

async def manager_with_progress(
    items: list[WorkItem],
    max_workers: int = 5,
) -> list[WorkResult]:
    sem = asyncio.Semaphore(max_workers)
    completed: list[WorkResult] = []
    failed: list[WorkResult] = []
    start = time.monotonic()

    async def tracked(item: WorkItem) -> WorkResult:
        result = await process_item(item, sem)
        if result.error:
            failed.append(result)
        else:
            completed.append(result)
        elapsed = time.monotonic() - start
        rate = len(completed) / elapsed if elapsed > 0 else 0
        print(f"\r  {len(completed)+len(failed)}/{len(items)} done  "
              f"({len(failed)} errors)  {rate:.1f} items/s", end="", flush=True)
        return result

    results = await asyncio.gather(*[tracked(item) for item in items])
    print()  # newline after progress line
    return list(results)
The right pool size depends on your API rate limit and per-request latency. A rough formula:
. In practice, start at 4–8, measure error rates (429s indicate you are over the limit), and adjust.

import pytest
from unittest.mock import AsyncMock, patch, MagicMock

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_manager_processes_all_items():
    items = [WorkItem(id=str(i), payload=f"text {i}") for i in range(6)]
    mock_resp = MagicMock()
    mock_resp.content = "summary"

    with patch("my_module.llm") as mock_llm:
        mock_llm.ainvoke = AsyncMock(return_value=mock_resp)
        results = await manager(items, max_workers=3)

    assert len(results) == 6
    assert all(r.output == "summary" for r in results)
    assert all(r.error is None for r in results)

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_manager_records_error_without_abort():
    items = [WorkItem(id="a", payload="ok"), WorkItem(id="b", payload="fail")]

    async def fake_invoke(messages):
        if "fail" in messages[-1]["content"]:
            raise RuntimeError("llm error")
        m = MagicMock()
        m.content = "ok result"
        return m

    with patch("my_module.llm") as mock_llm:
        mock_llm.ainvoke = fake_invoke
        results = await manager(items, max_workers=2)

    assert len(results) == 2
    ok = next(r for r in results if r.id == "a")
    bad = next(r for r in results if r.id == "b")
    assert ok.output == "ok result"
    assert bad.error is not None

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_semaphore_limits_concurrency():
    """Verify no more than max_workers coroutines hold the semaphore at once."""
    active = 0
    max_active = 0

    async def fake_invoke(_):
        nonlocal active, max_active
        active += 1
        max_active = max(max_active, active)
        await asyncio.sleep(0.01)
        active -= 1
        m = MagicMock(); m.content = "x"
        return m

    items = [WorkItem(id=str(i), payload="t") for i in range(10)]
    with patch("my_module.llm") as mock_llm:
        mock_llm.ainvoke = fake_invoke
        await manager(items, max_workers=3)

    assert max_active <= 3
Manager-worker is the right pattern for high-volume, uniform tasks where throughput and capacity control matter more than per-task routing.
Use asyncio.Semaphore for pure Python batch jobs; use LangGraph Send API when the manager-worker lives inside a larger pipeline.
Workers are stateless and homogeneous — one implementation handles all tasks.
Start max_workers at 4–8, watch for 429 errors, and tune based on your API tier.
Test that errors in individual workers don't abort the full batch, and verify concurrency caps are actually respected.
